channel.title: reproducible build environments on ilo
channel.description: Recent content in reproducible build environments on ilo

item.title: Alternatives
item.description: It’s highly likely that ilo does not provide the functionality you are looking for. Here is a list of alternatives that might cover your needs: toolbox: Toolbox is a tool that offers a familiar package based environment for developing and debugging software that runs fully unprivileged using Podman. gitian: Gitian is a secure source-control oriented software distribution method. rbm: Reproducible Build Manager (rbm) is a tool that helps you create and build packages for multiple linux distributions, and automate the parts that can be automated.

item.title: Argument Files
item.description: In order to share the same options/commands across your team, ilo supports argument files which contain the options for your project, e.g. which image you are using. Argument files are just plain text files and both name and location can be chosen at will. In order to use an argument file, you have to add @ in front of the file name: ilo @file-name. # write argument file $ cat some/folder/your-arguments.

item.title: autoenv
item.description: In order to simplify the usage of ilo, consider using run commands, argument files and a autoenv-like tooling. These tools all allow you to just enter a directory and will automatically call ilo for you like this: [you@hostname ~]$ cd path/to/your/project [root@container project-dir]# As soon as you enter the directory of your project, these tools will call ilo which in turn will open your build environment for you. autoenv In order to use autoenv, first install it and then place a .

item.title: Building ilo
item.description: ilo requires a certain set of software installed on your system in order to be built. Prerequisites git for version control minisign to sign the waiver as a contributor gpg to sign your the commit which adds the waiver Manual Setup Java JDK to compile the code Maven to build the project hugo in order to create the website GraalVM to build a native executable ilo Setup ilo to open the reproducible build environment for ilo itself One of the runtimes that ilo shell supports.

item.title: CI Servers
item.description: Jenkins Jenkins knows multiple ways to define jobs. In order to integrate with ilo make sure the ilo is installed on your slaves. Declarative Pipeline pipeline { stages { stage('Build with ilo') { sh 'ilo @build-things' } } } build-things is an arguments file.

item.title: CLI Autocomplete
item.description: The ilo generate-completion command generates autocompletion configuration for shells such as bash and zsh. In order to get autocomplete working in your shell, execute the following snippet: $ source <(ilo generate-completion) See the integration pages for various shells for details. Once enabled you can use the <TAB> key to autocomplete ilo commands and their options: # autocomplete commands $ ilo s<TAB> $ ilo shell # autocomplete options $ ilo shell --p<TAB> $ ilo shell --pull

item.title: Container Registries
item.description: In order to share build environments within a team, using a container registry is highly recommended. Docker Hub Docker Hub has an automated build system that can be used to automatically create a build environment which in turn can be used by team members of your project. Make sure Docker Hub rebuilds your build environment on every change to main (or any other branch) and have your contributors pull the resulting images to their machines.

item.title: Customize Environment
item.description: The ilo compose command looks for a docker-compose.yml file by default and uses the dev service to open your new shell. Specify any option, additional volumes, custom images and commands in that file in order to customize your build environment according to your needs. Depending on your preferred runtime you probably have to change the location of the file to use with the --file flag, e.g. pods-compose will look for a pods-compose.

item.title: Customize Environment
item.description: ilo allows you to define your build environment either in a Dockerfile or any other OCI Image compliant way. Make sure to specify your.image.here:some-tag as the image in your devcontainer.json file. Take a look at the reference documentation for all available options for that JSON file.

item.title: Customize Environment
item.description: The ilo shell command uses the fedora:latest image by default. In most cases fedora:latest will not be enough to compile/test/package/run your software. While you can install additional packages inside the container, those changes will be lost once you remove the container. In contrast to toolbox, ilo relies on immutable containers. ilo allows you to define your build environment either in a Dockerfile or any other OCI Image compliant way. Make sure your image can be accessed by everyone in your team and use ilo shell your.

item.title: Editors
item.description: IntelliJ IntelliJ Idea is a popular IDE by Jetbrains. It allows several ways to share settings across team members in a project. ilo stores its IntelliJ settings inside its git repository and thus should work out-of-the-box for other IntelliJ users. In case you have trouble getting ilo to work in IntelliJ, take a look at the help section.

item.title: Examples
item.description: The following examples show how ilo shell can be used. Cargo Projects Cargo caches all downloaded dependencies in your local ~/.cargo directory. In order to re-use already downloaded dependencies inside the container, specify a --volumne like this: # Gradle project that mounts local .gradle folder $ ilo shell \ --volume $HOME/.cargo:/root/.cargo:Z \ rust:latest Gradle Projects Gradle caches all downloaded dependencies in your local ~/.gradle directory. In order to re-use already downloaded dependencies inside the container, specify a --volumne like this:

item.title: First Timer
item.description: First time contributors have to perform an additional step in order to get their changes merged into the main repository. The WAIVER has to be signed in order to signal that all contributions can be published into the public domain using the Creative Commons Zero (CC0) license. minisign is used to sign and verify contributions (see dev-env). The signed waiver must be added to your first pull request. The created git commit must be GPG signed.

item.title: Git Mirrors
item.description: ilo is using GitHub as its primary source/issue/build environment, however its code is available in other git repositories as well in order to deal with GitHub outages. Available Mirrors Mirrors are updated automatically as explained in this blog post. https://github.com/metio/ilo https://gitlab.com/metio.wtf/ilo https://codeberg.org/metio.wtf/ilo https://bitbucket.org/metio-wtf/ilo https://repo.or.cz/ilo.git

item.title: Help
item.description: In case you need help, don’t panic - we’ve all been there! Try the following resources in order to get help: open a new bug report create a new feature request open a new discussion join the chat room send an email to the mailing list Take a walk outside & come back to your issue with a fresh mind

item.title: Help
item.description: In order to see the help information of ilo, run ilo --help or ilo -h. Its output looks something like this: $ ilo --help Usage: ilo [-hV] [@<filename>...] COMMAND Manage reproducible build environments [@<filename>...] One or more argument files containing options. Options: -h, --help Show this help message and exit. -V, --version Print version information and exit. Commands: shell Opens an (interactive) shell for your build environment compose Open an (interactive) shell using podman-/docker-compose devcontainer Open an (interactive) shell using devcontainer generate-completion Generate bash/zsh completion script for ilo.

item.title: Install
item.description: Use the following locations in order to download the binary distribution of ilo: https://github.com/metio/ilo/releases None of the widely used package managers have ilo in their official repositories yet. In case you want to help package ilo for your preferred operating system, take a look at the open packaging issues for Linux, MacOS, and Windows. Linux Manual The Linux distribution of ilo is a native executable and does not require any additional software to be installed.

item.title: Makefile
item.description: The Makefile commonly used task by maintainers of this project. In order to use it, you have to install a variant of make on your system. Just calling make in the root directory will print the help/usage guide: $ make usage: make [target] contributing: sign-waiver Sign the WAIVER example: ec-redis-java11 Example using 'ilo compose' w/ redis & java 11 es-default Example using 'ilo shell' w/ default settings es-openjdk11 Example using 'ilo shell' w/ openjdk11 es-maven Example using 'ilo shell' w/ maven hacking: build Build everything native-image Create a native image using GraalVM clean Clean build artifacts other: help Show this help Run any of the available targets like this:

item.title: Metadata
item.description: Every contributor may add/remove her/his metadata to the list of contributors at any time. Metadata is currently used in three places: To generate a list of existing minisign signatures in the first timer documentation. To generate a humans.txt file of all contributors. To generate a FOAF for the entire project. Adding a new entry Create a new file called <YOUR_NAME>.yaml in the contributors directory. Add the following properties to it:

item.title: News
item.description: In order to stay up-to-date, consider the following resources: Join the chat room Join the mailing list Subscribe to the release feed

item.title: Options
item.description: The ilo compose command can be configured with the following command line options. From your terminal, use ilo compose --help to get a list of all options, and their default values. --build The --build option can be used to build images before opening a new shell. Use this option in case you rely on local Dockerfiles. # build images before opening shell $ ilo compose --build # do not build images before opening shell $ ilo compose --build=false # do not build images before opening shell $ ilo compose By default, --build is not enabled.

item.title: Options
item.description: The ilo devcontainer command can be configured with the following command line options. From your terminal, use ilo compose --help to get a list of all options, and their default values. --debug The --debug option toggles whether ilo should print the runtime commands into your terminal before executing them. This can be useful in case you want to move away from ilo and just use your preferred runtime instead. # print runtime commands $ ilo devcontainer --debug # do not print runtime commands $ ilo devcontainer --debug=false $ ilo devcontainer By default, --debug is not enabled.

item.title: Options
item.description: The ilo shell command can be configured with the following command line options. From your terminal, use ilo shell --help to get a list of all options, and their default values. --context The --context option allows to specify the context when building an image with --dockerfile. # specify custom context $ ilo shell --context ./local/folder # do not specify context $ ilo shell By default, --context is set to . (the current directory).

item.title: Release
item.description: The release process of ilo is highly automated, therefore you only have to: Push changes into the main branch. Wait until an GitHub action will perform a release automatically. Take a look at the calendar for the next scheduled release. Each new release will: Publish a new GitHub release Publish an email Publish a toot Publish a post to reddit

item.title: Run Commands
item.description: In order to simplify/automate its usage, ilo will look for run command files in the following locations: .ilo/ilo.rc .ilo.rc Each file found will be added in-order as an argument file to your invocation of ilo before any other options you specify in your terminal.

item.title: Runtimes
item.description: ilo compose by default searches your local system for supported runtimes. It prefers docker-compose over podman-compose over pods-compose over footloose over vagrant at the moment. In order to force the usage of a specific runtime, use the --runtime flag. docker-compose Force ilo to use docker-compose like this: $ ilo shell --runtime docker-compose # alias $ ilo shell --runtime dc podman-compose Force ilo to use podman-compose like this: $ ilo shell --runtime podman-copose # alias $ ilo shell --runtime pc

item.title: Runtimes
item.description: ilo shell by default searches your local system for supported runtimes. It prefers podman over docker over lxd at the moment. In order to force the usage of a specific runtime, use the --runtime flag. Docker Force ilo to use docker like this: $ ilo shell --runtime docker # alias $ ilo shell --runtime d Important: In case your image uses root as its user, and you cannot run rootless docker (introduced with 19.

item.title: Security
item.description: Supported Versions ilo is using a continuous delivery mode and therefore only supports the latest released version. Reporting a Vulnerability Email security@metio.wtf with details about the vulnerability. Please include some way to reproduce your problem. We are going to provide a fix as soon as possible and include it in the next release.

item.title: Shells
item.description: In order to get the most out of ilo make sure it is well integrated into your shell. bash In order to integrate autocompletion into bash, follow these steps: Create or edit .bashrc. Add the following line source <(ilo generate-completion) Reload your shell (or create a new one) zsh In order to integrate autocompletion into zsh, follow these steps: Create or edit .zshrc. Add the following line source <(ilo generate-completion) Reload your shell (or create a new one) oh-my-bash In order to integrate autocompletion into oh my bash, follow these steps:

item.title: tmux
item.description: tmux users can simplify the usage of ilo with a tmux session manager. All of them allow you to configure reusable sessions that open project directories and execute commands for you. Consider combining them with run commands and/or argument files to simplify this even further. tmuxp In order to use tmuxp, first install it and then create a new configuration in $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/tmuxp: [you@hostname project-dir]$ cat $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/tmuxp/your-project.yml session_name: your-project windows: - window_name: dev window shell_command_before: - cd ~/path/to/your/project panes: - shell_command: - ilo @build-env teamocil In order to use teamocil, first install it and then create a new configuration in ~/.

item.title: Version
item.description: In order to see the version of ilo itself, run ilo --version or ilo -V. Its output looks something like this: $ ilo --version ilo: 0.0.0-SNAPSHOT JVM: 11.0.8 (Oracle Corporation Substrate VM GraalVM 20.2.0 Java 11) OS: Linux 5.7.17-200.fc32.x86_64 amd64
